retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Differential Diagnosis -- Microdontia
retrieved_docs.content: Pituitary dwarfism Hypopituitarism Growth hormone-related defects

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Macrodontia often presents an aesthetic concern in the anterior maxilla, while macrodonts in the posterior region may contribute to crowding and impede normal tooth eruption. Radiographic monitoring is essential to assess potential eruption abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During normal tooth development, various complications can occur that affect the number of developing teeth. These dental anomalies range from the absence of all teeth (anodontia) to having more teeth than the normal number of teeth (hyperdontia). When describing missing or absent teeth, some may also use the term agenesis, which is the failure of all or part of an organ to develop during embryonic growth. While mild cases of dental agenesis may not even be noticed until later in life, some forms of dental agenesis can be devastating and debilitating, resulting in a poor quality of life in affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the extent of anodontia, hypodontia, or oligodontia, different potential complications can arise. In patients missing most or all of their dentition, speech and mastication are generally affected, significantly impacting their quality of life. Patients missing 2 to 4 teeth may not be as severely impacted, as more restorative or prosthodontic treatment options are available.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental disturbances affecting the shape and size of teeth can significantly alter dental anatomy and necessitate tailored treatment. Some of these conditions primarily present as cosmetic concerns, while others complicate dental care, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Differential Diagnosis -- Macrodontia
retrieved_docs.content: Gemination Fusion Facial hemihypertrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Microdontia is a rare dental anomaly characterized by teeth that appear smaller than expected. True generalized microdontia, a condition affecting all teeth, often occurs in individuals with systemic syndromes, such as pituitary dwarfism and orofaciodigital or oculomandibulofacial syndrome. [1] [2] [3] Younger patients who have undergone chemoradiotherapy may also develop microdontia due to disruptions in dental development. [4] True generalized microdontia rarely occurs in individuals without underlying genetic or developmental conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Macrodontia, also known as megalodontia, has an unclear etiology, though both genetic and environmental factors have been implicated. [5] [6] Generalized macrodontia, where larger-than-normal teeth appear throughout the dentition, has been associated with conditions such as insulin-resistant diabetes, otodental syndrome, and hypophyseal gigantism. [7] In contrast, isolated macrodontia typically occurs without syndromic associations or underlying systemic pathology. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental dental anomalies related to tooth number, including anodontia, hypodontia, oligodontia, and hyperdontia, have varying clinical presentations. These anomalies can significantly impact oral health and function. These dental anomalies have the following manifestations: Anodontia refers to the complete absence of tooth development, resulting in the total failure of the dentition to form. Hypodontia involves the absence of 1 to 6 teeth and often serves as a general term to describe the lack of any number of teeth, regardless of quantity. Oligodontia denotes a more severe form of dental agenesis, characterized specifically by the absence of 6 or more teeth. Hyperdontia describes the presence of 1 or more additional teeth beyond the normal complement, leading to supernumerary tooth formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Anodontia, hypodontia, and oligodontia generally have a good prognosis for treatment outcomes, especially with early detection and diagnosis. Depending on the extent and number of missing teeth, various prosthodontic and restorative options are available. With severe hypodontia being associated with syndromes, management of the syndrome itself generally precedes and occasionally precludes management of the dentition.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Macrodontia results from the overexpression of developmental signals, leading to hyperactivity in the tissues involved in tooth formation. This anomaly has been linked to systemic, syndromic, and environmental factors and produces teeth that are larger than normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Odontogenesis is a complex, highly regulated biological process influenced by multiple factors. Disruptions can lead to anatomical changes and clinical complications, including altered tooth size, which can result in teeth that appear larger, smaller, or significantly different from their expected form. This activity reviews common anomalies affecting tooth shape and size, particularly microdontia, macrodontia, taurodontism, and fusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Agenesis of teeth often occurs in association with a syndrome and ranges from the absence of a single tooth to the failure of all the teeth to develop. Milder cases of dental agenesis can typically be restored or addressed by relatively noninvasive measures. Anomalies in the number of teeth are classified as anodontia, hypodontia, oligodontia, or hyperdontia based on the presence or absence of the number of teeth involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Microdontia refers to teeth that appear smaller than expected, and it is classified based on severity. Localized microdontia affects a single tooth. Relative generalized microdontia occurs when teeth appear smaller due to a disproportionately large maxilla or mandible. True generalized microdontia involves the entire dentition. [23] Macrodontia is often identified on routine dental radiographs between the ages of 8 and 13 when the permanent dentition begins to erupt. Isolated larger-than-normal teeth typically require no intervention, whereas generalized macrodontia should prompt further evaluation for an underlying genetic or developmental condition. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Measurement of Growth
retrieved_docs.content: Dental assessment: Primary tooth eruption begins with the central incisors at 6 months. No single tooth by 13 months of age is of concern. Permanent tooth eruption starts at 6 years of age and continues up to 18 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental dental anomalies related to tooth number, focusing on conditions, eg, anodontia, hypodontia, oligodontia, and hyperdontia. These anomalies can significantly impact oral health and function, ranging from the complete absence of teeth to the presence of supernumerary teeth. This course reviews the classification, etiology, clinical presentation, and diagnostic strategies associated with these conditions. Emphasis is placed on early detection and intervention, particularly in pediatric populations, to reduce long-term complications and enhance treatment outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of
